summary: Jeff Atwood argues that while touchscreens have their place, serious computer users—especially programmers—should appreciate and invest in quality mechanical keyboards. He contrasts the crippling text input experience on smartphones with the efficiency of proper keyboards, noting that most people unknowingly use inferior rubber dome keyboards. The post serves as both a love letter to mechanical keyboards and a practical guide for those wanting to explore this enthusiast space, covering switch types, keycap printing methods, and specific brand recommendations. Atwood acknowledges this borders on esoterica but maintains that anyone who values typing owes it to themselves to experience what a great keyboard feels like.
key_points: Touchscreen typing transforms users from content producers into passive consumers, which has concerning implications for a web built on user-generated content
Most keyboards today use cheap rubber dome or membrane switches that provide inconsistent, mushy feedback and degrade over time
Mechanical keyboards offer superior tactile feedback and durability but have become rare due to cost and public indifference
Choosing a mechanical keyboard involves considering noise level, tactile feedback, actuation force, key travel, and keycap printing method
While great keyboards aren't strictly necessary, those who love typing should experience what quality feels like
money_quotes: The minute I switch back to my regular keyboard, I go from being Usain Bolt to the Flash.
When was the last time you saw a hunt-and-peck pianist?
I can't take slow typists seriously as programmers.
The device that, when combined with the internet, turns every human being into a highly efficient global printing press.
What happens when people become trained to think of information and entertainment as something they receive and not something they create?
All this time, I've been typing on keyboards with least-common-denominator rubber dome innards.
The most important aspect of writing isn't the keyboard you use, but the simple act of getting out there and writing as much as you can.
key_insight: For people whose work is fundamentally about producing text, the quality of their keyboard matters far more than most realize, and the shift toward touch interfaces risks turning a generation of creators into mere consumers.
